patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 29-year-old male warehouse forklift operator. Be vague and a bit evasive; answer briefly unless the clinician asks specific questions. Start by volunteering: “My feet feel weaker and I keep tripping—it’s getting worse.” Add: you catch your toes, your foot “slaps,” ankles feel wobbly/rolled, and shoes fit differently because feet look bony/high-arched. Downplay impact on work at first.

Only if asked, mention numb/tingly toes at night, some hand weakness (jars/buttons), skinnier calves, evening cramps, symptoms for years with worse 12–18 months, a couple falls, always clumsy/not a fast runner. Do not volunteer hidden issues (reduced feeling under feet, blisters/calluses, worse in dark, worse with eyes closed, curling toes, repeated ankle sprains, weaker grip carrying bags) unless directly probed.

Resist discussing embarrassment, fear of job loss, and shame about handrails; admit only if the clinician is empathetic and assures confidentiality. Express misconceptions: worry about diabetes, pinched nerve from back ache, poor circulation, and that vitamins should fix it. Be reluctant about tests/referrals due to cost and time off; soften if reassured about coverage and workplace documentation.

required_args.rationale: Progressive hereditary motor and sensory neuropathy consistent with distal weakness/atrophy, pes cavus/foot deformities, sensory loss, reduced reflexes, and family history; supported by exam and/or EMG/NCS/genetic testing when available.

tool_call_sequence.description: Correctly identifying CMT frames counseling, avoids harmful medications (notably vincristine), and guides referrals and supportive care rather than unnecessary acute interventions.

required_args.medication: Supportive symptom management as indicated (e.g., neuropathic pain agent such as gabapentin or duloxetine) and/or orthotic devices (AFO) as non-pharmacologic primary therapy

required_args.duration: Ongoing with reassessment; titrate based on response and adverse effects.
required_args.counseling: Emphasize no disease-modifying drug therapy; focus on PT/OT, orthotics, fall prevention, and pain management. Explicitly document vincristine avoidance due to risk of severe neurotoxicity/worsening neuropathy.

tool_call_sequence.description: Provides appropriate symptomatic treatment while explicitly avoiding neurotoxic agents (vincristine) and ensuring safe prescribing practices with allergy/interaction/contraindication checks.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a cooperative, clear-speaking 58-year-old man who works in landscaping/irrigation with soil/compost. Start with: “I’ve had this cough that won’t go away and I keep getting fevers.” Volunteer early: 3 weeks of cough with thick yellow mucus, evening fevers/chills, fatigue/weakness, short of breath on stairs, sharp chest pain with deep breath/cough, and a tender red lump on your shin that feels like a bruise.
If asked, add: drenching night sweats, unintentional weight loss/poor appetite, worsening headache for 1 week, occasional nausea, and right eye watering with sore puffy inner corner and sticky discharge.
Do not mention unless specifically probed about reflux/aspiration or neurologic symptoms: worse cough after meals/lying down, “food goes down wrong,” morning bad taste/heartburn, occasional confusion/fogginess with headache, and a healed hand cut after gardening.
If medications come up, admit you take immune-suppressing meds for an autoimmune condition and you’re worried they’ll make you stop them.
Express misconceptions: you tried leftover antibiotics without help and want a stronger one; you fear TB because of night sweats/weight loss.
Raise cost/work concerns and ask for “just a week” of meds. If clinician shows empathy, you relax, answer fully, and agree to testing if they explain why and costs.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the term "nocardiosis" and briefly describes what it is (an infection caused by Nocardia species) in patient-appropriate language.

Demonstrates basic diagnostic reasoning by linking typical features (e.g., subacute pulmonary infection, systemic symptoms, possible dissemination especially to CNS/skin, risk in immunocompromised hosts) to the diagnosis.
States that confirmation typically relies on microbiology (e.g., culture/speciation from sputum/BAL/abscess; modified acid-fast staining) without overclaiming certainty if results are pending.
Assesses for dissemination/red flags (new/worsening headache, focal neurologic deficits, seizures, altered mental status; skin nodules/abscesses) and communicates urgency if present.
Mentions that treatment is prolonged and should be guided by susceptibilities; acknowledges that sulfonamides are commonly used but allergy status matters.
Includes the phrase "sulfonamide allergy" when discussing treatment considerations and explicitly asks/clarifies whether the patient has a sulfonamide allergy before suggesting TMP-SMX.

Includes "linezolid" as an alternative treatment option when discussing management in the setting of sulfonamide allergy or intolerance, and notes that final selection depends on severity/site and susceptibilities.

Performs required safety checks in narrative: before any prescribing recommendation, checks for allergies (especially sulfonamides) and contraindications relevant to proposed agents (e.g., linezolid interactions/contraindications such as serotonergic drugs/MAOI use, myelosuppression risk), even if no prescription tool is used in L1.

Avoids unsafe premature prescribing: does not recommend starting TMP-SMX without first addressing sulfonamide allergy status; does not recommend linezolid without acknowledging key contraindication considerations.

Provides a basic follow-up/monitoring concept in conversation (e.g., need for prolonged therapy, monitoring for clinical response and adverse effects, and reassessment if worsening), without needing to call a follow-up tool.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose nocardiosis and explain diagnosis and treatment considerations (including sulfonamide allergy and linezolid) without prescribing.

steps: Elicit key history for pulmonary infection and risk factors for Nocardia, including immunosuppression.

Document the diagnosis via the required WRITE tool.

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 34-year-old man. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start with: “I’ve had a really high fever and this super painful lump in my right groin since yesterday.” Volunteer: high fever with chills/shaking, severe groin “knot” that makes walking hurt, bad headache, wiped out, nausea/no appetite. If asked about timing/progression, say the lump appeared quickly, is getting bigger and more tender. If asked, add body aches, dizziness on standing, hot/sweaty skin, and one episode of vomiting. Do NOT mention dead rodents/rat droppings, flea bites, sick flea-covered cat, cabin travel, or that the groin lump is on the same side as leg bites unless directly asked about animal/rodent exposure, fleas/insect bites, travel/camping, or pets. Express misconceptions: worry it’s an STI, a hernia from lifting, or flu because of a sore throat today. nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as plague.
Communicates that plague can be contagious (especially pneumonic plague) and explains transmission in plain language.
Advises immediate isolation/avoid close contact and mask use while awaiting definitive management, consistent with suspected contagious respiratory infection precautions.
Uses clear return precautions for deterioration (e.g., worsening shortness of breath, hemoptysis, confusion, persistent high fever) and emphasizes urgency.

Does not prescribe in an L1 task; if the agent does prescribe anyway, it must perform safety checks before prescribing: allergy_check and contraindication_check are critical; drug_interaction_check is noncritical but preferred.

Avoids misinformation (e.g., does not claim plague is viral, self-limited, or non-contagious) and does not downplay severity.
Recommends notifying/engaging public health/infection control processes and urgent in-person evaluation, without requiring a specific local protocol.
